By the meeting, I had pinpointed the culprit on the timeline.
We had assessed the impact and started the rollback.
In the aftermath, we made a plan to mitigate the risk.
I had already escalated it when the engineer arrived.
By the time he logged in, we had restored the service.
First, it spiked. Then, it crashed. After that, I escalated it.
An old script had triggered the problem before we noticed it.
First, it crashed. By the time the engineer arrived, I had escalated it. Finally, we restored it.
Daniela, let us go through the timeline. What had happened by the time the engineer arrived?
By the time he arrived, I had already escalated the issue and we had assessed the impact.
Good. So what was the order of events?
First, the traffic spiked. Then, the service crashed. After that, we started the rollback.
And the root cause? Had you found it before the meeting?
Yes. Before the meeting started, I had pinpointed the culprit: an old script had triggered the memory problem.
Excellent. And the aftermath?
In the aftermath, support was busy, but no data was lost. Finally, we made a plan to mitigate the risk.
First it spiked, then it crashed, and after that I escalated it.
Before the meeting, I had pinpointed the culprit.
Finally, we made a plan to mitigate the risk.

Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about sequencing the past. When you tell a story, two past actions do not always happen at the same time. Sometimes one happens before the other. For the earlier action, the first one in time, we use the past perfect. The form is easy. Use had, plus the past participle. I had escalated the issue. We had already restored the service. Then, for the later action, we use the past simple. I had escalated the issue when the engineer arrived. See the order. First, I escalated. Then, he arrived. The past perfect shows what had happened before. To make the order even clearer, use sequencers. First, then, after that, and finally. First, the traffic spiked. Then, the service crashed. After that, I escalated it. Finally, we found the culprit. So, use the past perfect for the earlier event, and sequencers to put the story in order. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. I am leaving you a quick voicemail about the postmortem meeting. So, here is the full timeline of last night. First, the traffic spiked at nine. Then, the payment service crashed. By the time I logged in, you had already escalated the issue, which was great. After that, the on call engineer arrived. When he arrived, we had already assessed the impact and started the rollback. Finally, we restored the service and ran a quick check. This morning, before the meeting started, I had pinpointed the culprit. An old script had been running for weeks and had triggered the memory problem. We have a plan to mitigate it. In the aftermath, customer support had a busy hour, but no data was lost. Good work last night, Daniela. Call me back when you can. Talk soon.
